source_data.prob_desc_notes: NoteIn the first testcase, the optimal strategy is as follows:   Subtract 1 from x (9 → 8) paying 3 coins.  Divide x by 2 (8 → 4) paying 1 coin.  Divide x by 2 (4 → 2) paying 1 coin.  Divide x by 2 (2 → 1) paying 1 coin. The total cost is 6 coins.In the second test case the optimal strategy is to subtract 1 from x 4 times paying 8 coins in total.
source_data.prob_desc_input_spec: The first line contains a single integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 2·109). The second line contains a single integer k (1 ≤ k ≤ 2·109). The third line contains a single integer A (1 ≤ A ≤ 2·109). The fourth line contains a single integer B (1 ≤ B ≤ 2·109).
source_data.prob_desc_output_spec: Output a single integer — the minimum amount of coins you have to pay to make x equal to 1.
source_data.prob_desc_description: Right now she actually isn't. But she will be, if you don't solve this problem.You are given integers n, k, A and B. There is a number x, which is initially equal to n. You are allowed to perform two types of operations:   Subtract 1 from x. This operation costs you A coins.  Divide x by k. Can be performed only if x is divisible by k. This operation costs you B coins.  What is the minimum amount of coins you have to pay to make x equal to 1?
